class_schedule.topic: HTML Foundations: Semantic Markup for Designers
learning_objectives: Write semantic HTML that communicates document structure to browsers and assistive technology
Understand the relationship between HTML structure and visual design implementation
Build a well-structured web page from a design mockup using semantic elements
readings: HTML & CSS: Design and Build Websites by Jon Duckett, Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: HTML is the structural foundation of every web interface you will ever design. Understanding HTML is not about becoming a developer; it is about becoming a designer who can communicate precisely with developers, build functional prototypes, audit accessibility, and ensure designs are implementable. Every Figma component you create will eventually become HTML elements. Knowing which elements exist, what they communicate to browsers and assistive technology, and how they constrain layout possibilities makes you a fundamentally better designer. Semantic HTML is also the accessibility layer: screen readers navigate by landmarks (nav, main, footer), not by visual appearance. A page that looks correct but uses only div elements is inaccessible to the 15% of the population that uses assistive technology.
key_concepts: Semantic HTML elements: header, nav, main, section, article, aside, footer communicate document structure and purpose
Document structure: proper heading hierarchy (h1 through h6); only one h1 per page; headings create a navigable outline
Forms: input types (text, email, number, date, range), labels, fieldsets for grouping, and their UX implications for mobile keyboards
Links vs buttons: anchor elements navigate to URLs, button elements trigger actions; using the wrong one breaks keyboard behavior
HTML as the accessibility layer: proper markup enables screen reader navigation by landmarks without any additional ARIA attributes
Lists: ul for unordered groups, ol for ordered sequences, dl for term-description pairs; semantic lists improve screen reader navigation
Images: alt text describes image content for screen readers; decorative images use empty alt to be skipped; informative images need descriptions
Tables: only for tabular data, never for layout; th for headers with scope attribute for complex tables
lecture_notes.demonstration: Convert a Figma design mockup of a product landing page into semantic HTML, discussing element choices and accessibility implications at each step, then test with a screen reader
setup_requirements: VS Code with Live Server extension

review.value: Using <nav> instead of <div class='nav'> communicates meaning. Screen readers announce 'navigation landmark' for nav but say nothing useful for div. Semantic HTML is the foundation of web accessibility.
Links navigate to a URL. Buttons trigger an action on the current page. Using the wrong element breaks keyboard behavior (Enter vs Space) and screen reader expectations.
h1 > h2 > h3 creates a navigable document outline. Screen reader users jump between headings to scan content. Heading level matches document structure, not visual size.
Informative images need descriptive alt: 'Bar chart showing 30% enrollment increase'. Decorative images use alt='' to be ignored by screen readers.

class_schedule.topic: CSS Layout: Flexbox and Grid for Designers
learning_objectives: Build responsive layouts using CSS Flexbox and Grid matching Figma auto-layout designs
Translate Figma auto-layout properties directly to CSS Flexbox properties
Implement responsive breakpoints using media queries and fluid techniques
readings: CSS-Tricks: A Complete Guide to Flexbox
CSS-Tricks: A Complete Guide to Grid
Every Layout by Andy Bell and Heydon Pickering

lecture_notes.introduction: CSS Flexbox and Grid are the layout tools that bring your Figma designs to life in the browser. Figma auto-layout is literally a visual implementation of CSS Flexbox. If you understand auto-layout, you already understand Flexbox conceptually. Grid extends this to two-dimensional layouts for complex page structures. Together, they handle every layout pattern in modern web design. Understanding them lets you design layouts that are naturally responsive and avoids proposing layouts that are impossible or expensive to implement. This knowledge bridges the gap between visual design and technical implementation.
key_concepts: Flexbox: one-dimensional layout (row OR column) mapping directly to Figma auto-layout; display: flex, flex-direction, justify-content, align-items, gap
Grid: two-dimensional layout (rows AND columns) for page structures; display: grid, grid-template-columns, grid-template-rows
Responsive design: media queries change layout at breakpoints; mobile-first approach starts with single column
CSS custom properties: --spacing-md: 1rem creates reusable design tokens; the CSS equivalent of Figma variables
Box model: margin (outside), border, padding (inside), content; box-sizing: border-box makes sizing predictable
Flexbox alignment: justify-content distributes along main axis, align-items along cross axis
Grid template areas: named layout regions matching wireframes for intuitive page structure
Container queries: components respond to their container size for truly reusable responsive components
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a responsive card grid adapting from 1 column (mobile) to 3 columns (desktop) using CSS Grid, then build a complete page layout with header, sidebar, and main content using Grid template areas

review.value: Figma auto-layout with Space Between = CSS justify-content: space-between. Packed with center alignment = justify-content: center. This 1:1 mapping improves designer-developer collaboration.
Grid handles the page skeleton (header, sidebar, main, footer) while Flexbox handles component internals. Use both together for complete responsive pages.
Start CSS with mobile layout then add complexity with min-width media queries. Simpler code, better mobile performance, matches CSS cascade naturally.
Without it, padding adds to width. With border-box, padding is included inside the specified width. Always set globally.

class_schedule.topic: CSS Styling: Typography, Color, and Design Tokens
learning_objectives: Implement typography systems from Figma design tokens to CSS custom properties
Apply color palettes and spacing scales using a CSS token architecture
Build a complete design token system maintaining correspondence with Figma variables
readings: Refactoring UI by Wathan and Schoger
MDN Web Docs: CSS Custom Properties

lecture_notes.introduction: CSS is where your design tokens become real code. Every design decision in your Figma file has a direct CSS equivalent. CSS custom properties create a single source of truth for these decisions, just like Figma variables. When the brand color changes, update one CSS variable and the entire site updates. When the client wants dark mode, redefine token values under a data-theme attribute. This session bridges the gap between design files and live code by implementing the exact design token systems you create in Figma, establishing a workflow that scales from prototype to production.
key_concepts: CSS custom properties: --color-primary: #2563EB declared once, used everywhere with var(--color-primary)
Typography tokens: --font-family-body, --font-size-body, --line-height-body; complete type scale in variables
Color tokens: semantic names (--color-text-primary, --color-surface) rather than raw hex values; enables theming
Spacing scale: --spacing-xs through --spacing-3xl; consistent spacing derived from a base unit
Typography CSS: font-family stacks with fallbacks, font-size in rem for accessibility, line-height as unitless multiplier
Color formats: hex for simplicity, HSL for programmatic manipulation, RGB for opacity
The cascade and specificity: how CSS determines which style wins when multiple rules apply
Dark mode: redefine token values under a theme attribute for instant theme switching
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement a complete design token system from a Figma spreadsheet, build a card component and button set using only token values, then toggle dark mode by switching one attribute

review.value: Figma variable color/primary: #2563EB becomes CSS --color-primary: #2563EB used as color: var(--color-primary). Every design token has a direct CSS equivalent.
CSS applies styles based on specificity then source order. Understanding specificity prevents the frustrating situation where styles do not apply as expected.
rem is relative to root font size and respects user preferences. px is fixed. Use rem for text and spacing; it enables accessibility for users with larger font settings.
HSL separates hue, saturation, and lightness. Hover states: just reduce lightness. Much easier than computing new hex values.

class_schedule.topic: Interactive CSS: Transitions, Animations, and States
learning_objectives: Implement hover, focus, active, and disabled states using CSS pseudo-classes
Create smooth transitions and purposeful keyframe animations
Build accessible focus indicators using focus-visible
readings: Animation at Work by Rachel Nabors, Ch. 5-6
MDN Web Docs: CSS Transitions and Animations

lecture_notes.introduction: Interactive states and motion bring your interaction design specifications to life. Every interactive element needs multiple states: default, hover, focus, active, disabled. CSS transitions smooth changes between states. Keyframe animations handle multi-step sequences. Understanding what CSS can animate performantly (transforms and opacity) versus what stutters (width, height) is essential for specifying smooth animations. Accessibility requires visible focus indicators for keyboard users and the ability to disable animations for users with vestibular disorders. Today we build components that are both polished and universally accessible.
key_concepts: CSS pseudo-classes: :hover, :focus, :focus-visible (keyboard only), :active, :disabled for interactive states
CSS transitions: transition: background-color 200ms ease-in-out smoothly animates between states
Keyframe animations: @keyframes for multi-step sequences like loading spinners
Performance: transform and opacity are GPU-accelerated at 60fps; width, height, margin cause layout recalculation
Reduced motion: @media (prefers-reduced-motion) respects users with vestibular disorders
Focus-visible: shows focus rings only for keyboard navigation, not mouse clicks
Timing functions: ease, ease-in-out, cubic-bezier() for custom motion curves
State layering: default, then hover, focus-visible, active in cascade order
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a button component with all states (default, hover, focus-visible, active, disabled, loading spinner), a card with hover lift effect, and a navigation menu with smooth dropdown transitions

review.value: Only transform and opacity animate smoothly at 60fps. Animating width, height, or margin triggers layout recalculation and stutters. Always prefer transform for movement animations.
:focus-visible shows focus styles only for keyboard navigation. Solves the design conflict between visual polish (no click rings) and accessibility (visible keyboard focus).
200-300ms for UI state changes. 150ms for instant feedback. Above 500ms feels sluggish. Below 100ms is imperceptible.
Some users experience nausea from animation. This media query lets you disable or reduce motion for those users. Essential for accessibility.

learning_objectives: Use JavaScript to add real interactivity beyond what Figma prototyping can achieve
Handle user events including clicks, form inputs, and keyboard interactions
Manipulate the DOM to show, hide, and update content dynamically
readings: MDN Web Docs: Introduction to Events

lecture_notes.introduction: JavaScript adds dynamic behavior to web pages. For UX designers, basic JavaScript enables building interactive prototypes with real data, real input validation, and real application behavior. A Figma prototype simulates clicking through screens; a JavaScript prototype filters search results, validates forms with feedback, persists selections across pages, and fetches data from APIs. This fidelity generates higher-quality user testing insights. You are learning enough JavaScript to build convincing prototypes and understand implementation possibilities when designing interactions.
key_concepts: DOM manipulation: querySelector selects elements; textContent changes text; classList.toggle shows/hides elements
Event handling: addEventListener for click, input, change, keydown, submit events on interactive elements
Conditional logic: if/else implements interactive behavior based on user state and input values
Variables and data: const and let store data; arrays and objects organize collections
Template literals: backtick strings with embedded variables for dynamic HTML content generation
Form handling: preventDefault stops submission; reading input values for validation and processing
Fetch API: loading data from JSON files for data-driven prototypes with realistic content
Local Storage: persisting user data between page loads for multi-step flows
Error handling: try/catch prevents JavaScript errors from breaking the entire prototype
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build an interactive search prototype: typing filters results in real time, clicking a result shows detail in a side panel, category filters narrow results, and a clear button resets everything, all using data from a JSON file

review.value: JavaScript responds to user actions through event listeners: element.addEventListener('click', handler). Every interactive UI element needs an event listener.
Change content: element.textContent = 'Updated'. Toggle visibility: element.classList.toggle('hidden'). Create elements: document.createElement('div'). This is how dynamic interfaces work.
Selects elements by CSS selector: document.querySelector('.search-input'). Same selectors from CSS work in JavaScript.

Backtick strings embed data: `

${item.title}

` creates HTML from JavaScript variables. Essential for rendering dynamic content.

learning_objectives: Build fully responsive layouts adapting from mobile to desktop viewports
Implement mobile-first CSS with progressive enhancement through breakpoints
Test responsive behavior using browser tools and real devices
readings: Responsive Web Design by Ethan Marcotte

lecture_notes.introduction: Responsive design ensures interfaces work on every device from 320px phones to 2560px monitors. Understanding responsive CSS reveals the constraints and possibilities of multi-device design. Designing at fixed Figma frame sizes does not prepare you for the fluid nature of real browser resizing. Breakpoints are only part of the story; fluid typography, flexible images, and intrinsic sizing create layouts that look good at every width. Today we build layouts that truly respond to their environment rather than snapping between three fixed states.
key_concepts: Mobile-first: write CSS for smallest screen first, add complexity with min-width media queries
Media queries: @media (min-width: 768px) adds columns at wider viewports
Fluid typography: clamp(1rem, 2.5vw, 2rem) scales between minimum and maximum based on viewport
Flexible images: max-width: 100%; height: auto prevents overflow while maintaining aspect ratio
Viewport units: vw, vh, dvh for dynamic viewport height on mobile
Container queries: components respond to container size for reusable responsive behavior
Touch targets: minimum 44x44px for mobile; adequate spacing prevents mis-taps
Testing: browser emulation, real device testing, responsive mode for all widths
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a complete responsive page: mobile hamburger navigation, fluid typography, adaptive card grid (1 to 4 columns), and responsive form layout. Test at every width from 320px to 2560px.

review.value: Starting mobile means simpler base styles, progressive enhancement, faster mobile loading, and natural content hierarchy.
font-size: clamp(1rem, 1rem + 1vw, 1.5rem) scales text smoothly without media queries. Eliminates sudden typography jumps at breakpoints.
Components adapt to their container width rather than viewport width. A card in a sidebar uses compact layout; the same card in main content uses full layout.
640px, 768px, 1024px, 1280px as starting points. Test your specific content to find where natural breaks occur.

learning_objectives: Organize CSS using BEM naming convention for maintainable class names
Build a reusable component library from design system specifications
Implement design token integration at the component level
readings: CSS Architecture for Modern Web Applications
Atomic Design by Brad Frost, Ch. 2-3

lecture_notes.introduction: As prototypes grow, CSS organization becomes critical. Without a system, CSS becomes fragile where changing one style breaks something else. Component-based architecture organizes styles around UI components rather than pages. BEM naming provides predictable class names communicating structure. Combined with CSS custom properties for tokens, this produces maintainable CSS that maps to your Figma component library. Today we build a component library in CSS mirroring the structure of a design system.
key_concepts: BEM: .card (block), .card__title (element), .card--featured (modifier); flat specificity, predictable names
Component files: one CSS file per component imported into main stylesheet; organized like Figma components
Token integration: components reference tokens exclusively, never hardcoded values
State classes: .button--loading, .card--selected for component states via modifier classes
Utility classes: .visually-hidden, .text-center for one-off adjustments; use sparingly
Specificity management: flat single-class selectors prevent cascade conflicts
Component composition: atoms into molecules into organisms following Atomic Design
CSS custom properties for variants: component-level theming without duplicating rule sets
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a 10-component CSS library: button (3 variants, 4 states), card, form input (with label and error), navigation, badge, avatar, alert, modal overlay, tab group, and breadcrumb

review.value: .search-form (block), .search-form__input (element), .search-form__input--error (modifier). Flat specificity. Self-documenting. No cascade conflicts.
button.css, card.css, main.css imports all. Mirrors Figma component organization. Makes styles findable.
Never hardcode colors or sizes. Always reference variables. When tokens change, components update automatically.
If you need !important, your architecture has a problem. BEM keeps everything at equal specificity. Prevention beats !important.

class_schedule.topic: Advanced JavaScript: Components and Data-Driven UI
learning_objectives: Build reusable JavaScript UI components for tabs, modals, and accordions
Load and render data dynamically from JSON files for realistic prototypes
Implement state management for multi-view applications
readings: You Don't Know JS Yet: Scope and Closures

lecture_notes.introduction: Building on JavaScript fundamentals, this session covers reusable interactive components and data-driven prototypes. Real applications load data from servers and render it dynamically. Your prototypes should too for authentic testing. Common UI patterns (tabs, modals, accordions) appear in virtually every web application. Understanding how they work in code helps you design them better and communicate implementation requirements precisely.
key_concepts: Reusable components: JavaScript functions that initialize behavior on any matching element
Tab component: active state management, panel switching, keyboard navigation with arrow keys
Modal component: opening, closing, focus trapping, Escape key to dismiss, scroll lock
Accordion: expanding/collapsing sections, aria-expanded for accessibility
Data fetching: fetch loads JSON data for dynamic rendering
Dynamic rendering: template functions create HTML from data objects
State management: JavaScript object tracking application state with re-rendering on changes
URL parameters: encoding filter state in URLs for shareable prototype states
ES modules: import/export for organizing JavaScript across files
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a product catalog: load 50 products from JSON, render filterable card grid with category tabs, implement real-time search, add detail modal on card click, use URL parameters for shareable filter states

review.value: function initTabs(container) finds tabs within a container, adds listeners. Call once per tab group. Reusable across pages.
When a modal opens, Tab must cycle within modal content only. Essential for keyboard accessibility in overlay components.
Map data to HTML templates and insert. When filters change, re-render with filtered data. The data-driven approach of modern web apps.
const state = { activeTab: 'all', query: '' }. Centralizing state makes complex interactions manageable.

learning_objectives: Implement WCAG 2.1 AA requirements in HTML, CSS, and JavaScript
Test with keyboard navigation, screen readers, and automated tools
Build accessible forms with proper labeling and error handling

lecture_notes.introduction: Accessibility is a fundamental quality of good UX, not an optional addition. WCAG 2.1 Level AA is the legal and professional standard. As a designer who codes, you uniquely catch accessibility issues that pure designers miss (no real code to test) and developers miss (no UX understanding). Every prototype from this point should be keyboard-navigable, screen-reader-compatible, and contrast-compliant.
key_concepts: Keyboard navigation: every interactive element reachable and operable; Tab to navigate, Enter/Space to activate, Escape to dismiss
Color contrast: WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text
ARIA attributes: aria-expanded, aria-label, role; use only when native HTML is insufficient
Accessible forms: visible labels, aria-describedby for error messages, aria-required
Focus management: move focus when content changes dynamically to prevent disorientation
Skip links: hidden links to main content for keyboard users bypassing navigation
Live regions: aria-live announces dynamic changes without focus change
Automated testing: axe DevTools catches common violations; manual testing catches the rest
Screen reader testing: VoiceOver or NVDA verify the actual assistive technology experience
lecture_notes.demonstration: Audit a prototype with axe DevTools, fix all issues, manually test with keyboard and VoiceOver, fix focus management in modal, add form labeling, implement skip links, verify contrast

review.value: Unplug your mouse and navigate. Can you reach everything with Tab? Activate with Enter/Space? Dismiss with Escape? If not, the prototype is not accessible.
First rule: do not use ARIA if native HTML provides the semantics. Button is better than div with role=button. Native HTML has built-in keyboard behavior.
Run on every page to catch missing alt text, contrast failures, missing labels. Passing axe does not mean fully accessible, but failing means definitely not.
Modal opens: focus to modal. Modal closes: focus back to trigger. Tab changes: focus to new panel. Prevents keyboard users from getting lost.

learning_objectives: Use CSS nesting and preprocessing for efficient styling at scale
Set up a modern front-end development workflow
Understand design-to-code tooling connecting Figma tokens to CSS

lecture_notes.introduction: Modern front-end development uses tools that enhance CSS and streamline workflows. CSS nesting is now native in browsers. Sass adds preprocessing features. Tailwind CSS offers utility-first styling. Design-to-code tooling like Style Dictionary generates CSS from Figma tokens automatically. Understanding these tools helps you collaborate with development teams and build sophisticated prototypes.
key_concepts: CSS nesting: .card { .card__title { } } reduces repetition; now native in modern browsers
Sass: variables, nesting, mixins for reusable patterns, file organization with partials
Utility-first CSS: Tailwind approach for rapid prototyping with atomic classes
Token pipeline: Figma tokens exported as JSON, transformed to CSS custom properties
Build tools: Vite for fast development with hot reload
CSS modules: locally scoped class names preventing conflicts in larger projects
Component libraries: Shadcn/UI, Radix provide accessible unstyled components
Performance: minification, unused CSS purging, critical CSS extraction
lecture_notes.demonstration: Set up a Vite project with Sass, create a token file generating CSS custom properties, build components with Sass features, compare BEM, Sass, and Tailwind approaches

review.value: Group related styles under parent selector. Native in modern browsers without Sass. Reduces repetition.
Sass extends CSS with programming features. Tailwind replaces writing CSS with composing utility classes. Both are valid; choice depends on team and project.
Export Figma tokens, transform with Style Dictionary, generate CSS/SCSS/iOS/Android files. One source of truth across platforms.
Modern build tool with instant hot reload, Sass support, and production builds. Standard front-end development tooling.

class_schedule.topic: Multi-Page Prototypes with Navigation and State
learning_objectives: Build multi-page applications with consistent navigation and layout
Implement client-side navigation patterns including breadcrumbs and step indicators
Use localStorage to persist data across prototype pages
readings: Sprint by Jake Knapp, prototyping chapter
MDN Web Docs: Web Storage API

lecture_notes.introduction: Real applications have multiple pages connected by navigation. Multi-page prototypes test navigation mental models, information architecture, and flow completion in ways single-page prototypes cannot. Maintaining consistent headers, navigation state, and user data across pages requires localStorage. Today we build a complete multi-page prototype with shared layout, persistent state, and breadcrumb navigation.
key_concepts: Multi-page structure: shared layout with page-specific content
Navigation patterns: top bar, sidebar, breadcrumbs, tabs, step indicators
Active state management: highlighting current page in navigation
localStorage: persisting form data and selections between pages
Multi-step forms: storing partial data at each step, displaying on review page
Breadcrumb navigation: generated from URL path showing user location
URL parameters: encoding state for shareable prototype links
Page transitions: CSS transitions enhancing continuity between pages
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a 5-page e-commerce checkout: product listing, product detail, cart, checkout (3 steps), and confirmation. Cart data persists in localStorage. Step indicators show progress. Breadcrumbs track the journey.

review.value: setItem saves data between pages. getItem retrieves it. Enables realistic multi-page flows without a server.
Consistent header and nav on every page. JavaScript injection keeps them synchronized without copy-paste.
Visual progress for multi-step flows. CSS classes for active and complete states. Updates as user advances.
Not everything needs building. Simulate payment processing. Build form validation that gives real feedback. Focus on testing the experience.

learning_objectives: Understand how performance affects UX metrics and user satisfaction
Implement image, font, and CSS performance best practices
Measure Core Web Vitals using browser tools

lecture_notes.introduction: Performance is a UX concern. A 5-second load page has 90% bounce rate versus 10% for 1-second loads. Users on mobile networks experience sites very differently. Understanding performance means designing interfaces implementable at acceptable speeds. Image formats, font loading, CSS architecture, and JavaScript size all affect the real user experience.
key_concepts: Core Web Vitals: LCP (largest content paint), FID (first input delay), CLS (cumulative layout shift)
Image optimization: WebP/AVIF formats, responsive srcset, lazy loading, proper sizing
Font optimization: font-display: swap, preloading critical fonts, subsetting
CSS performance: critical CSS inlined for above-fold, deferring non-critical stylesheets
JavaScript deferral: defer attribute, minimizing critical path JavaScript
Layout shift prevention: width/height on images, aspect-ratio CSS, reserving space
Perceived performance: skeleton screens, progressive loading, optimistic UI
Lighthouse audit: automated scoring with improvement recommendations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Audit a slow prototype with Lighthouse, fix top 3 issues (optimize images, defer CSS, add font-display: swap), re-measure and show before/after improvement

review.value: Measures when largest visible element loads. Target: under 2.5 seconds. Optimize hero images and critical CSS first.
Measures unexpected layout movement. Target: under 0.1. Set dimensions on images and use font-display: swap.
WebP is 30% smaller than JPEG. Proper sizing avoids serving 4000px images in 400px containers. Lazy load below-fold images.
A skeleton screen in 200ms feels faster than white page for 2 seconds. Perceived speed matters more than actual speed for satisfaction.

learning_objectives: Implement a design system as a coded component library
Document components with usage guidelines and accessibility requirements
Understand design-to-code tooling bridging Figma and production CSS
readings: Design Systems by Alla Kholmatova, Ch. 5-7

lecture_notes.introduction: Design systems bridge design and development. A Figma design system defines components visually; a coded system implements them as reusable patterns. When aligned, designers and developers speak the same language. As a designer who codes, you can build the coded library matching your Figma system, ensuring implementation matches design. Today we build a documented, reusable, accessible component collection.
key_concepts: Component documentation: name, variants, usage guidelines, accessibility requirements, code examples
Token-to-code pipeline: Figma tokens to JSON to CSS custom properties consumed by components
Component variants: modifier classes for different visual treatments of the same component
Composition: combining atoms into molecules into organisms following Atomic Design
Accessibility per component: keyboard behavior, ARIA attributes, focus management documented
Component states: default, hover, focus, active, disabled, loading, error for every interactive element
Versioning: documenting changes and migration paths for evolving components
Governance: processes for adding and reviewing design system components
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a 6-component coded design system: button (4 variants), text input (with label and error), card, badge, alert, and modal. Document each with usage guidelines and accessibility notes.

review.value: Consistency (every button identical), speed (compose from existing), accessibility (accessible by default), quality (tested once, reliable everywhere).
Figma token to exported JSON to CSS custom property to component class. One source of truth flowing through the entire system.
Name, description, when to use, variants, accessibility requirements, and live code example. Good documentation drives adoption.
Compose pages from existing components. If customization is needed, create a variant rather than overriding the existing component.

learning_objectives: Deploy prototypes to shareable URLs using Netlify
Conduct usability tests with deployed code prototypes
Analyze findings and translate them into design improvements
readings: Rocket Surgery Made Easy by Steve Krug

lecture_notes.introduction: The ultimate purpose of code prototypes is testing with real users. A deployed prototype at a URL can be tested on any device without special software. Participants interact with something behaving like a real product, generating higher-fidelity insights. Today we deploy prototypes, conduct tests, analyze results, and translate findings into specific improvements, completing the design-build-test-iterate cycle.
key_concepts: Netlify deployment: drag-and-drop folder, receive shareable URL in seconds, free for static sites
Usability test planning: define tasks not questions, recruit 3-5 participants, prepare script with scenarios
Remote unmoderated testing: share URL and task list; participants complete independently with screen recording
Think-aloud protocol: participants narrate thoughts revealing mental models and confusion
Task metrics: success rate, time on task, error rate, satisfaction per task
Analysis: compile observations, identify patterns (3+ participants same issue = real problem), prioritize by severity
Translating findings: specific design changes, code changes, content changes with priority ranking
Iterative testing: fix top issues, re-test with new participants; rapid iteration produces better results than one large study
lecture_notes.demonstration: Deploy e-commerce prototype to Netlify, conduct live usability test with 2 class participants, analyze findings, implement top 3 improvements in real time

review.value: Create account, drag project folder, receive URL. No servers, no configuration, no cost for static prototypes.
3-5 participants per round with quick iteration between rounds produces the best research ROI.
Tasks reveal behavior; opinions reveal preferences. Behavior is more reliable for design decisions.
Problem found by 4/5 participants preventing task completion = critical. Minor issue found by 1 = backlog. Prioritize by impact times frequency.

learning_objectives: Present a complete interactive prototype demonstrating all course skills
Evaluate peer prototypes through code review and accessibility audit
Articulate how front-end skills enhance UX design practice

lecture_notes.introduction: This final session celebrates the transformation from designers who hand off static mockups to designers who build. Your presentations demonstrate implementing design systems, responsive layouts, accessible components, and deployed prototypes. These skills make you more effective: you understand constraints, prototype at higher fidelity, audit production code, and communicate precisely with developers. Your portfolio now includes deployed, interactive web experiences demonstrating both design thinking and technical capability.
key_concepts: Portfolio presentation: deployed URL, design decisions, technical implementation, test findings
Code review: peer evaluation of HTML semantics, CSS architecture, JavaScript, accessibility
Accessibility audit: axe and keyboard testing on peer prototypes
Career positioning: design engineering, UX engineering, front-end design roles
Skill inventory: semantic HTML, CSS layout, tokens, interactivity, responsive, accessibility, deployment
Continuing development: React/Vue, TypeScript, Storybook, Figma Dev Mode
Design-development collaboration: speaking both languages bridges implementation quality gaps
lecture_notes.demonstration: Instructor demonstrates presentation format and peer review rubric with sample project

review.value: You do not replace developers. You augment design with technical understanding. Better prototypes, better specs, earlier accessibility catches, more effective collaboration.
Show Figma design and coded implementation side by side. Demonstrates design thinking plus technical implementation capability.
HTML semantics, CSS layout, tokens, typography, interactive states, animations, JavaScript, responsive design, accessibility, components, deployment, testing.
UX Engineer and Design Technologist roles bridge design and development. Combined Figma plus code portfolio positions you for these high-demand roles.

quizzes.topic: HTML Semantics, CSS Layout, and Design Tokens

questions.question: A designer hands you a Figma mockup with a navigation bar containing 5 items spaced evenly across the top of the page. Which CSS layout method would you use, and why? Write the key CSS properties.
questions.answer: Use CSS Flexbox because this is a one-dimensional layout (items in a row). CSS: nav { display: flex; justify-content: space-between; align-items: center; padding: 0 var(--spacing-lg); }. Flexbox maps directly to Figma auto-layout with Space Between distribution. Preferred over Grid because the layout is single-axis.

questions.question: Explain why CSS custom properties are better than hardcoded values for a design system. Give a practical example.
questions.answer: Custom properties provide a single source of truth. Instead of #2563EB in 47 places, define --color-primary once. When the brand color changes, update one line. Also enables dark mode by redefining tokens: [data-theme='dark'] { --color-primary: #60A5FA; }. Maps directly to Figma design token workflow.

rubric.key: Mentions theming or design token connection

questions.question: What is the accessibility difference between nav and div with a class of nav? Explain the impact on screen reader users.
questions.answer: nav is a semantic landmark. Screen readers announce it as 'navigation' and list it in the landmarks menu for quick jumping. div with a nav class has no semantic meaning; screen readers treat it as a generic container. Users must navigate through every element to find navigation. Semantic elements create navigable structure for assistive technology users.

quizzes.topic: Interactive CSS, JavaScript, and DOM Manipulation

questions.question: Explain the difference between CSS :focus and :focus-visible. Why should designers care? When would you use each?
questions.answer: :focus applies on keyboard Tab AND mouse click. :focus-visible applies only for keyboard navigation. Designers care because: removing :focus to eliminate click rings also breaks keyboard accessibility. :focus-visible solves both: keyboard users see the ring, mouse users do not. Use :focus-visible for buttons and links. Use :focus for form inputs where the ring is always helpful.

rubric.key: Guidance on when to use each

questions.question: Write JavaScript pseudocode for a Show More button that reveals hidden content and changes to Show Less. Identify the DOM methods used.
questions.answer: Select button and content with querySelector. Add click event listener. In handler: toggle 'hidden' class with classList.toggle. Update textContent based on whether hidden class is present. The hidden class uses display: none in CSS. This toggle-a-class pattern is the fundamental show/hide mechanism on the web.

questions.question: Name three GPU-accelerated CSS properties and three that are not. Explain why this matters for animation specifications.
questions.answer: GPU-accelerated: transform, opacity, filter. Not accelerated: width/height, margin/padding, background-color. Animating width causes layout recalculation every frame, causing stutter on mobile. The same effect via transform: scaleX() runs at smooth 60fps. Designers must specify animations using transform and opacity for performance.

rubric.key: Why this matters for animation specs

quizzes.topic: Components, Responsive Design, and Data-Driven UI

questions.question: Explain BEM naming convention with a card component example. Why is BEM preferred over nested selectors?
questions.answer: BEM: Block__Element--Modifier. Example: .card (block), .card__title (element), .card--featured (modifier). Preferred because: flat specificity prevents cascade conflicts, self-documenting names, predictable targeting, no unintended style matches on nested components.

questions.question: Write CSS for a responsive card grid: 1 column mobile (below 640px), 2 columns tablet, 3 columns desktop (above 1024px). Mobile-first approach.
Describe how to build an accessible tab component. Include HTML structure, ARIA attributes, and keyboard behavior.
questions.answer: Container has role=tablist. Each tab is a button with role=tab, aria-selected, aria-controls. Panels have role=tabpanel, aria-labelledby. Arrow keys move between tabs. Home/End go to first/last. Active tab shows its panel. This follows WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices.

questions.question: An axe audit finds: missing alt text on an image, a form input without a label, and insufficient button contrast (2.8:1). For each, explain impact and fix.
questions.answer: Missing alt: screen readers read filename meaninglessly. Fix: add descriptive alt text. Missing label: screen readers say 'edit text' with no context. Fix: add label element with for/id linking to input. Low contrast (2.8:1): text hard to read for low vision users; AA requires 4.5:1. Fix: darken text or lighten background until ratio exceeds 4.5:1.

questions.question: Explain LCP and list three techniques to improve it. Connect each to a designer's decisions.
questions.answer: LCP measures when the largest visible element loads. Target: under 2.5s. (1) Optimize hero image: WebP format, proper sizing. Designer choice of a 5MB photo vs optimized WebP affects LCP by seconds. (2) Preload fonts: font-display: swap. Choosing 4 weights vs 2 affects font download time. (3) Inline critical CSS: simpler above-fold designs with fewer unique styles load faster.

questions.question: Write HTML and CSS for a button with primary, secondary, and ghost variants using BEM and CSS custom properties. Shared structure, variant-only color changes.
Describe the workflow for deploying a prototype to Netlify and conducting a remote usability test. Include deployment, test planning, and analysis.
questions.answer: Deploy: ensure index.html at root, drag folder to Netlify, receive URL, verify all pages work. Plan: define 4-5 tasks, recruit 3-5 participants, write script with scenarios. For remote unmoderated: share URL, tasks, and recording instructions. Analyze: note completion rates and errors per task, identify patterns (3+ participants = real issue), prioritize by severity times frequency, create improvement tickets.

questions.question: Write JavaScript to save shipping form data on step 2 and display it on a review page (step 4) using localStorage.
questions.answer:
Step 2 save: form.addEventListener('submit', function(e) { e.preventDefault(); const data = { name: document.getElementById('name').value, address: document.getElementById('address').value }; localStorage.setItem('shipping', JSON.stringify(data)); window.location.href = 'step3.html'; });
Step 4 retrieve: const shipping = JSON.parse(localStorage.getItem('shipping') || '{}'); document.getElementById('review-name').textContent = shipping.name || 'Not provided';

questions.question: Identify three ways front-end development skills make you a more effective UX designer, with concrete examples from this course.
questions.answer: (1) Designing implementable layouts: understanding Flexbox/Grid means I design layouts mapping directly to CSS, avoiding expensive workarounds. (2) Higher-fidelity prototypes: JavaScript prototypes with real data and validation generate better test insights than Figma click-throughs. (3) Early accessibility catches: knowing button vs div semantics means I identify issues in design review, not after development.

major_assignments.title: Responsive Design System Implementation
major_assignments.description: Implement a complete responsive web page from a Figma design using semantic HTML, CSS custom properties as design tokens, Flexbox/Grid layouts, and interactive states. Must be responsive from 320px to 2560px, pass axe accessibility audit with zero violations, and include a token system mapping to Figma variables.
objectives: Translate Figma design into semantic, accessible HTML
Implement CSS design token system matching Figma variables
Build responsive layout with mobile-first approach
Create interactive states for all interactive elements
deliverables: Semantic HTML implementing complete Figma design
CSS files with BEM naming and component organization
CSS custom properties matching Figma tokens
Responsive verification at mobile, tablet, desktop

criteria: Forms use proper labels and input types
CSS custom properties for all token values
Layout matches Figma at all breakpoints
Semantic accessible HTML from Figma design
CSS token system with Figma mapping

major_assignments.title: Interactive JavaScript Prototype
major_assignments.description: Build a multi-page interactive prototype with dynamic JSON data, JavaScript components (tabs, modal, search/filter), form validation, localStorage persistence, and deployment to a shareable URL. Conduct usability testing with 2-3 participants.
objectives: Build interactive prototype with data-driven content
Create form validation with real-time feedback
deliverables: Form with validation and error states

criteria: Test report with tasks and findings

major_assignments.title: Capstone: Coded Design System and Prototype
major_assignments.description: Build a coded design system (8+ components) with documentation and a multi-page prototype demonstrating the system. Includes tokens, accessible components, responsive behavior, documentation, deployment, usability testing, and class presentation.
objectives: Create multi-page prototype using the system
Deploy, test, and present with design rationale
deliverables: 8+ coded components with variants and states
Token CSS file with semantic naming
Deployed URLs for system and prototype

criteria: Usage guidelines and accessibility per component

weekly_quizzes.description: Five quizzes on HTML, CSS, JavaScript, accessibility, and front-end development

lab_assignments.description: Weekly coding exercises building front-end skills with increasing complexity

major_assignments.description: Three projects: responsive implementation, interactive prototype, and capstone design system

midterm_examination.description: Practical exam: implement a responsive accessible component from a Figma specification

final_project.description: Capstone design system and prototype with live presentation

course_policies.attendance: Lab sessions involve live coding. Missing labs means missing foundational skills. More than 3 absences affect grades.
course_policies.late_submissions: Labs build sequentially. 10% penalty per day for up to 5 days. Deployed prototypes must function at live URL.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion and debugging help encouraged. All code individually written. Copy-paste from tutorials without understanding prohibited.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Code must be your own or attributed. AI tools permitted for learning but generated code must be understood and modified. Unmodified AI code is prohibited.
course_policies.technology_requirements: VS Code with Live Server, modern browser with dev tools, Netlify or GitHub account, Figma for design reference.
